messages.content: improve the scientific writting of the follow text: These results are in accordance with the feeding ecology of Liza ramada and above all, reflect the trophic plasticity of this fish family (Koussoroplis et al., 2010, Moyo 2016, Cardona 2016, Carassou 2016). While in the estuary, the FA profile support the higher benthic-detritivorus foraging habits described (Blaber, 1987, Almeida et al., 2003), in freshwater denotes the typical active filter-feeding activity that is not directed towards a specific group of microalgae, but rather at the predominant group (Romer &McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996) and includes organic matter from urban effluents (major source of the microbial FA)  (Almeida 1996)

These findings are consistent with the feeding behavior of Liza ramada and demonstrate the remarkable trophic flexibility exhibited by this fish species (Koussoroplis et al. 2010, Moyo 2016, Cardona 2016, Carassou 2016). The fatty acid profiles observed in the estuary were indicative of a benthic-detritivorous diet, agreeing with previous reports (Blaber 1987, Almeida et al. 2003). Conversely, in freshwater habitats, Liza ramada displayed a typical active filter-feeding behavior that did not target a particular microalgal group, but rather relied on the most abundant group (Romer & McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996), including organic matter from urban effluent, which is a substantial source of microbial fatty acids (Almeida 1996).

While in the estuary, the FA profile support the higher benthic-detritivorus foraging habits described (Blaber, 1987, Almeida et al., 2003), in freshwater denotes the typical active filter-feeding activity that is not directed towards a specific group of microalgae, but rather at the predominant group (Romer &McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996) and includes organic matter from urban effluents (major source of the microbial FA)  (Almeida 1996).

In the estuary, the fatty acid profile supports the previously reported higher benthic-detritivorous foraging behavior of Liza ramada (Blaber 1987, Almeida et al. 2003). However, in freshwater habitats, this species exhibited the expected active filter-feeding activity, which was not targeted towards a specific microalgal group but towards the most abundant group (Romer & McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996). This feeding behavior also included organic matter sourced from urban effluent, which constitutes a significant source of microbial fatty acids (Almeida 1996).

in freshwater denotes the typical active filter-feeding activity that is not directed towards a specific group of microalgae, but rather at the predominant group (Romer &McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996) and includes organic matter from urban effluents (major source of the microbial FA)  (Almeida 1996).

In freshwater habitats, Liza ramada exhibited their typical behavior of active filter-feeding, which was not selective towards a specific microalgal group, but rather targeted the predominant group (Romer & McLachlan 1986, Almeida 1996). Additionally, this behavior included consumption of organic matter from urban effluents, which was identified as a significant source of microbial fatty acids (Almeida 1996).

In this sense, it reinforces that seasonal habits shifts lead to temporal dietary changes, that are highly dependent up on the food resources available at site, and major differences in fish condition and FA composition are expected to occur (Carassou 2016).

This highlights the fact that seasonal changes in the behavior of Liza ramada result in temporal variations in their diet, which are heavily influenced by the availability of food resources at their habitat. Consequently, significant differences in fish condition and fatty acid composition are expected to occur (Carassou 2016).

So, under unpredictable environmental conditions and stochastic events, species migratory behaviour seems to promote a conditional reproductive strategy with capital-income breeders that attempt to maximize adult fitness in response to the environmental conditions and achieve a balance between system productivity and fishery productivity [14].

Under uncertain environmental conditions and stochastic events, the migratory behavior of species serves to facilitate a conditional reproductive strategy that features both capital-income breeders. This strategy aims to maximize adult fitness in response to these variable environmental conditions, and strives for a balance between system productivity and fishery productivity [14].

Species migratory behaviour seems to promote a conditional reproductive strategy of capital-income breeders that allow to maximize adult fitness in response to the environmental conditions and achieve a balance between system productivity and fishery productivity. This may explain the extended spawning period described across species distribution and the existence of a protracted recruitment, that ultimately, contribute to a genetic homogenization and maintenance of a unique species stock. If individual’ migratory behavior or breeding pattern shift with ontogeny or environmental conditions remains unknown, but migration into freshwater water is a significant life-cycle component of a population fraction, with impacts on fitness, and hence population maintenance.

The migratory behavior of species appears to facilitate a conditional reproductive strategy that involves capital-income breeders, enabling them to maximize their adult fitness in response to environmental conditions and maintain a balance between system productivity and fishery productivity. This may account for the prolonged spawning period observed across species distribution and the presence of an extended recruitment phase, which ultimately contributes to genetic homogeneity and the maintenance of a unified species stock. It remains unclear if individual migratory behavior or breeding patterns change with ontogeny or environmental conditions, but the migration into freshwater is a significant component of the life cycle for a proportion of the population. This migration has potential impacts on fitness and, therefore, on the preservation of the species.

Still, one must not forget that besides the advantages inferred based on the comparative analysis of fatty acid profiles, the freshwater contingent benefits from a lower competition for food resources and predation from piscivorous fish (Carassou 2011).

It is essential to note that, in addition to the benefits inferred from the comparative analysis of fatty acid profiles, the freshwater population also enjoys lower competition for available food resources and reduced susceptibility to predation by piscivorous fish (Carassou 2011).

On the other side, the estuarine contingent seems to maintain a fine-tuning reproductive output relative to the estuarine and marine environment, which in turn allows to take advantage of temporal windows of suitable conditions to both adults and potential survival of the offspring. These strategy is commonly associated to fish species inhabiting tropical and subtropical regions with relatively constant environmental conditions that are likely to fulfil their energy requirements for reproduction by concurrent feeding. Which explained the higher estuarine residence observed in the Mediterranean sea when compared with species behaviour in the Atlantic region where it display a more a migration with more defined contours (2, 16 inDhurmeea 2018).

The estuarine population, however, appears to maintain a finely-tuned reproductive output, which is well-suited to the estuarine and marine environment. This strategy enables them to take advantage of temporal windows of favorable conditions for both adult fish and potentially their offspring. This behavior is often observed in fish species that inhabit tropical and subtropical regions with relatively constant environmental conditions, which allow them to fulfill their energy requirements for reproduction through concurrent feeding. This may explain the higher degree of estuarine residency observed in the Mediterranean Sea when compared to species behavior in the Atlantic region, which typically involves more defined migratory patterns (Dhurmeea 2018, 2,16).
The estuarine contingent appears to maintain  a fine-tuning reproductive output relative to the estuarine environment, with metabolism and physiological maturity stages synchronized.
It seems that the estuarine population maintains a finely-tuned reproductive output that is synchronized with the estuarine environment, including metabolic and physiological maturity stages.
The estuarine contingent appears to maintain  a fine-tuning reproductive output relative to the estuarine environment, with metabolism and physiological maturity stages synchronized.
It seems that the estuarine population maintains a finely-tuned reproductive output that is synchronized with the estuarine environment, including metabolic and physiological maturity stages.

On the other hand, the estuarine contingent seems to maintain a fine-tuning reproductive output relative to the estuarine and marine environment, which in turn allows to take advantage of temporal windows of suitable conditions to both adults and potential survival of the offspring

On the other hand, the estuarine population appears to synchronize its reproductive output with the estuarine and marine environment, enabling them to take advantage of temporal windows of favorable conditions for both adult fish and potential offspring survival.

On the other hand, the estuarine contingent seems to maintain a fine-tuning reproductive output (physiological and metabolic) relative to the environment, which in turn allows to take advantage of temporal windows of suitable conditions to both adults and potential survival of the offspring.

On the other hand, the estuarine population appears to finely tune its reproductive output, including physiological and metabolic aspects, according to the prevailing environmental conditions. This strategy enables them to maximize their success during temporal windows of favorable conditions for both adult fish and potential offspring survival.

This reproductive strategy is often observed in fish species that inhabit tropical and subtropical regions with relatively constant environmental conditions, which allow them to fulfill their energy requirements for reproduction through concurrent feeding (income breeders). This may explain the higher degree of estuarine residency observed in the Mediterranean Sea when compared to species behavior in the Atlantic region, which typically involves more defined migratory patterns

This type of reproductive strategy is commonly observed in fish species that inhabit tropical and subtropical regions with relatively constant environmental conditions, which enable them to meet their energy requirements for reproduction through concurrent feeding (income breeders). This phenomenon may explain the higher degree of estuarine residency observed in the Mediterranean Sea when compared to species behavior in the Atlantic region, where more defined migratory patterns are typically observed.

Opposite to the FA profile of muscle, in summer, Chelon ramada gonads showed high expression of unsaturated FA and n-3 family FA, where the most relevant HUFA for gonadal maturation and reproductive success, such as EPA and especially DHA, are included. While a similar fraction of PUFA was attributed to EPA (23% to 28%), in freshwater, the gonad presented almost twice the fraction of DPA, half of the ARA (expression of 2.55%), 3.7 times less DHA (expression of 2.64%) and a higher ratio of EPA/DHA (3.63% against 1.03%), when compared with the estuary. Which suggests that despite the presence of long chain FA (C18 and C20FA), also supported by the trophic analysis as higher expression of “macroalgae marker”, among the EFA, there’s a clear predominance of FA from the initial metabolic pathway of n-3 family (DHA precursors) and a disequilibrium of EPA/DHA.

In contrast to the fatty acid profile observed in muscle, Chelon ramada gonads expressed high levels of unsaturated fatty acids and n-3 family fatty acids during the summer season. This included EPA and DHA, which are considered to be the most relevant highly-unsaturated fatty acids for gonadal maturation and reproductive success. While a similar proportion of polyunsaturated fatty acids were attributed to EPA (23% to 28%), the freshwater gonads expressed almost twice the amount of DPA, half the amount of ARA (at 2.55%), 3.7 times less DHA (at 2.64%), and a higher ratio of EPA/DHA compared to the estuary. These results suggest that, despite the presence of long chain fatty acids (C18 and C20), which were supported by the trophic analysis as higher expression of "macroalgae marker," there is a clear predominance of fatty acids from the initial metabolic pathway of n-3 family (DHA precursors) and a disequilibrium of EPA/DHA within the gonads.

Thus, although the freshwater contingent had a larger gonad volume and higher lipid and energy content, metabolically, the EFA present in lipid pool are not consistent with the more advanced, and macroscopically identified, maturity stages (predominance of stage IV). Whereas mullets from the estuary, macroscopically identified, almost entirely, in stage III of maturation, and with lower GSI and total lipid content, showed a higher advance in terms of the metabolic pathways related with reproduction.

Therefore, although the freshwater population had larger gonad volumes and higher lipid and energy contents, the essential fatty acids present in the lipid pool were not consistent with the more advanced maturity stages that were identified macroscopically (predominance of stage IV). Meanwhile, the mullets from the estuary, which were almost entirely identified as being in stage III of maturation, and had lower gonadosomatic index (GSI) and total lipid content, displayed a higher level of advancement in terms of the metabolic pathways associated with reproduction.

Whereas mullets from the estuary, macroscopically identified, almost entirely, in stage III of maturation, and with lower GSI and total lipid content, showed a higher advance in terms of the metabolic pathways related with reproduction.

In contrast, the mullets from the estuary were predominantly identified as being in stage III of maturation, with lower GSI and total lipid content, but exhibited a more advanced status in terms of the metabolic pathways related to reproduction.

Among the available studies on mullet’ FA composition, most are focused on the flathead grey mullet and were developed under the context of human nutrition or aquaculture production. Moreover, several factors such as species, nutrition , catching season, maturity, temperature and salinity are known to influence the FA profile, thus, when available data exists, any comparison requires caution.

Most of the available studies regarding the fatty acid composition of mullet have focused on the flathead grey mullet and were typically conducted within the context of human nutrition or aquaculture production. However, it is important to note that several factors, including species, nutrition, catching season, maturity, temperature, and salinity, can all have an impact on the fatty acid profile. Therefore, any comparisons made between studies must be made with caution and should be based on available relevant data.

Regarding the low expression of DHA found in freshwater, Carassou et al 2017 found a similar discrepancy when investigating the ontogenetic and seasonal variations in the diet of the Myxus capensis across a river–estuary interface.

A similar discrepancy in the low expression of DHA was found by Carassou et al. 2017 when investigating ontogenetic and seasonal variations in the diet of Myxus capensis across a river-estuary interface.

Chelon ramada has the typical reproductive cycle of autumn-spawning species, which is characterized by a very long maturity stage III, that take place between winter to the next autumn, with the most active accumulation of lipids in summer, and a very short stage V, that in the specific case, is only achieved at sea. Thus, a natural delay of ovary development, close to the phenomenon of dormancy, will enable the adults to exploit and take advantage of the warm and food-rich summer/autumn period and adjust spawning and juvenile appearance to the most favorable conditions

Chelon ramada displays the typical reproductive cycle of autumn-spawning species, characterized by a long stage III of maturity that occurs from winter to the following autumn. The most active accumulation of lipids takes place during the summer season, followed by a very short stage V that is only achieved at sea. This natural delay in ovarian development, which is reminiscent of dormancy, allows adults to exploit the food-rich and warm summer/autumn season and adjust spawning and juvenile appearance to the most favorable environmental conditions.

Besides the environmental cues such as the photoperiod and water temperature, dietary oils are known to modulate the teleost pituitary and gonadal hormone levels, with implication in gonad development and reproductive performance [23–28 in Sorbera2001]. The result of the present study provide evidence that with Chelon ramada migration to freshwater, the dietary shift to higher total n-7 PUFA, associated with higher microalgae and phytoplankton intake, metabolic changes with repercussion on gonad maturation allowed to increase muscle and gonad lipid reserves in 1.9 and 4.8 times, respectively, and 1.05 times the muscle energy, when compared to the brackish water contingent.

In addition to environmental cues such as photoperiod and water temperature, dietary oils are known to modulate teleost pituitary and gonadal hormone levels, which have implications for gonad development and reproductive performance (Sorbera2001, 23-28). The results of this study provide evidence that, with the migration of Chelon ramada to freshwater, a dietary shift to higher total n-7 PUFA, associated with increased microalgae and phytoplankton intake, triggered metabolic changes that impacted gonad maturation. This was reflected in an increase of lipid reserves in muscle and gonads of 1.9 and 4.8 times, respectively, and a 1.05 times increase in muscle energy compared to the brackish water population.  Overall, this suggests that dietary factors play a significant role in the reproductive success of Chelon ramada.
